name: LinPEAS — Linux Privilege Escalation
description: Comprehensive Linux privilege escalation enumeration script. Checks SUID binaries, capabilities, cron jobs, writable paths, kernel exploits, and more.

arguments.| sh: Pipe directly to shell (or save and run)

use_case: After gaining initial shell, run LinPEAS to find privilege escalation vectors automatically.
prerequisites: Ability to download or transfer script
common_mistakes: Not running as the compromised user (run as current user, not root)
Missing output due to terminal issues (use tee)

name: WinPEAS — Windows Privilege Escalation
description: Comprehensive Windows privilege escalation enumeration. Checks services, registry, tokens, scheduled tasks, unquoted paths, and more.

arguments.winPEASx64.exe: Run the 64-bit version (use x86 for 32-bit systems)

expected_output:
╔══════════╣ Unquoted Service Paths
C:\Program Files\Vulnerable Service\service.exe
╔══════════╣ AlwaysInstallElevated
Enabled!
use_case: After gaining initial Windows shell, enumerate all possible privilege escalation vectors.

name: Find SUID Binaries
description: Find all binaries with the SUID bit set — these run as the file owner (often root) regardless of who executes them.
use_case: Identify SUID binaries that can be abused for privilege escalation. Check each against GTFOBins.
detections: Process running as root from non-standard path
common_mistakes: Forgetting to check GTFOBins for each found binary

name: Check Sudo Permissions
description: List what commands the current user can run with sudo. Often reveals misconfigured sudo rules that allow privilege escalation.

arguments.-l: List allowed (and forbidden) commands for the invoking user

expected_output:
User www-data may run the following commands:
    (ALL) NOPASSWD: /usr/bin/vim
    (root) NOPASSWD: /usr/bin/python3
use_case: First thing to check after getting a shell — sudo misconfigurations are the most common Linux privesc vector.
remediation: Follow least privilege for sudo rules
Never use NOPASSWD for dangerous binaries
Use sudo with specific command arguments
common_mistakes: Not checking GTFOBins for allowed commands

name: Kernel Exploit Suggester
description: Identifies potential kernel exploits based on the target's kernel version. Suggests CVEs with available exploits.
expected_output:
Kernel: 5.4.0-42-generic
[+] CVE-2021-4034 (PwnKit) — Likely vulnerable
[+] CVE-2022-0847 (DirtyPipe) — Likely vulnerable
use_case: When other privesc methods fail, check if the kernel itself is vulnerable to known exploits.
common_mistakes: Running exploit without understanding it (may crash system)
Not checking if exploit matches exact kernel version
